Thanks!

entry.title: SAP Digital Manufacturing Production Process Quality — Blog Series, Part 2
entry.content:
Purpose of this series

This three-part series distils best practices from SAP Digital Manufacturing implementation projects to raise the quality, maintainability, and speed of your Production Process Designs (PPDs):

  • Part 1: Foundations, naming conventions, and design discipline (dry but essential). LINK
  • Part 2 (this post): Structuring production processes, designing scalable machine integrations, and optimizing your designs for performance.
  • Part 3: Bits and pieces that may change with DM releases (transport options, evolving patterns, practical tips). This, in the end, will serve as a repository for smaller blogs on specific Production Process topics that come up over time.

 

Introduction to structuring production processes

In the first part of the series, at this point, we introduced criticality levels (must, should, could, ...) to later weigh the different guidelines against each other. However, this blog will rather share approaches to build good production processes, and depending on the business context, you will need to decide for yourself which ones are the most important. The reason for this is that some of the approaches will contradict each other, so it depends on what is more important in your business challenge. Let me give an example: for reusability and maintainability, it. On the other hand, this leads to the fact that there are calls between the production processes, handing over data and receiving the results, which gives a small performance overhead. In most cases, you would choose the reusable variant, but if you need the last milliseconds of performance or you are facing a requirement which only arises in one very specific business context, you may decide against the modularization.

As you still need to weigh the arguments, this is still some more advanced input on the PPDs, but should still also help novice users to gain insights and weigh different approaches. In the end, you should be able to understand this sample PPD structure, which covers many scenarios. We will cycle back to it in the last section.

 

Why structure matter

In the first part of the blog, we will mostly discuss common structuring approaches. So, we enable all the great things we already told you about, well-structured PPDs in the first part. As a little reminder, these are accelerated delivery, lower risk, and easier operations:

  • Support teamwork: Clear separation of responsibilities reduces coordination effort and enables ownership.
  • Fast, stable deployments: Smart structuring avoids long deployments and timeouts and helps you stay within environment limits.
  • Easier imports/transports to PROD: Encapsulating asset interactions simplifies re-imports and limits manual adaptations.
  • Easy operation: Better understandability, maintainability, and testability; changes are confined to small, well-defined designs.

To enable all these goals, we will try to reuse production processes as much as possible and build them in a modularized fashion. We will start from the beginning by identifying usage areas to then discuss how to reuse designs build for the different usage areas.

 

PPD usage areas

Let's start by finding good PPD structures by examining common usage areas. The usage areas are a good first criterion for grouping and structuring production processes. These areas should help you identify common classes of functions found in most SAP DM implementations, in some form. Sometimes there is a greater focus on one or the other, but this depends on the industry and the general use scenario.

DM business functions & business system integration processes. Machine integration focuses on integrating with assets and PLCs, reading and writing tags, and often handling counters or delta calculations. DM business functions encompass helper logic, UI interactions you automate, and things you automate within DM. Examples are printing, pre-complete checks, custom functions for POD buttons and so on. Business system integration extends the integration to systems like ERP, EWM or Success Factors or others. Here you typically find automations of this integration or deeper integration into further modules like equipment creation on the end of production, integration of HR data of a 3rd party HRM system, integration of inspection lot 04, staging automation, etc.

 

Trade-offs of generalization levels

As we found some solution areas to build the first production process designs, the next question is where we can use those designs in the business context of DM. Do we face tenant-wide designs, or in other words, designs which are used in all plants, or do we have plant or even production line-specific designs? For this consideration, we would like to introduce some trade-offs. You must be aware that all processes in the same design share the same lifecycle, so they are deployed, moved, and undeployed together. This means if you create more generic designs that are e.g. valid across multiple plants, you will face the followin

  • Possible bugs in the design may occur in all plants at the same point in time. So, a bug has a higher impact.
  • The new version goes live across all plants at the same time, so if you need preconditions like no active orders or a production break, that requires more coordination.
    Further, the different plants might be in different phases of DM implementation. When you only implement one plant at a time this should still be rather easy to manage, but if you implement multiple plants in parallel you should have an eye on this to prevent unintended side effects.
  • In the design, you need to consider more business scenarios, as plants are usually somewhat different, and you need to account for all process variants. This sounds complex, but when you add plant after plant in a rollout is well manageable.
  • Performance can be a challenge for cross-plant designs, but it does not need to be. As the cross-plant process needs to cover more variants, it will involve more decisions, making it more difficult to optimize for a specific scenario. On the other hand, since it serves a wider scope, it is worthwhile to spend a bit more time optimizing it.

On the other hand, with more generic designs, you also gain a lot

  • You will have the same behaviour across plants. Further processes can build on this, and it is easier to understand.
  • No need for multiple adoptions in case of changes; if the business process is changed, an API needs replacement, or a bug is found. Everything can be fixed centrally
  • Over multiple rollouts of DM the processes will harden, meaning fewer and fewer bugs will be found, and more and more scenarios will be covered. In the last rollouts, they will likely need no adjustments, and you will gain speed.
  • Last but no least, generic PPs will be one of the main factors to gain seed in your DM rollout. As you don’t need to develop your specific business logics again and again, but you only configure proper master data, automatic triggers and possible configuration data of your PPDs. This will be a tremendous boost over multiple plants.

As a rule of thumb, always go for the cross-plant design and only make it plant-dependent if there are urgent reasons to do so.

If you decide a plant-specific design is needed, because of one of the trade-offs you could still consider copying the previous plant's version as the base version. That way, you should end up with similar processes and still gain some speed.

 

Storing Configuration

In the previous section we argued mostly for generic processes. If you follow this direction, you will quickly notice that configuration is needed for your production processes. This configuration will describe how the process should behave in certain plants and/or scenarios. Here are some examples for these configurations:

  • You want to store the path to the indicators of the machine for a given work center and plant. So that you can use the information in read-/write- indicators flexibly.
  • You have built a production process checking multiple data objects before allowing an SFC complete, and you want to configure which objects the process should check (e.g., open DC, open NC, open Assemblies)

In SAP Digital Manufacturing, there are several possible places to store configuration data. The following table will present and compare the available options so you can select one for your needs.

 

Machine integration processes

By now, you should be familiar with the basic structuring trade-offs and the typical PPD usage areas. In the next sections, we will look at each area 1-by-1 to discuss them further and provide more detailed recommendations. Machine Integrations are one of the main effort drivers for SAP Digital Manufacturing implementation projects. Therefore, it is very important to try to reuse the configuration you built for this.  At the same time, it is very difficult to reuse it completely, as machines offer different APIs/Tags and have different protocols for how data needs to be integrated. The approaches discussed here represent only one part of the overall picture. It is important to consider establishing a machine integration standard for your production. This means that when you acquire new machines or upgrade their interfaces, you should aim to implement a common integration technology and protocol. We understand that achieving this can be challenging or, in some cases, not feasible. However, it's essential to keep this in mind as you develop your machinery. Having machines that share a common communication pattern and interface technology will make integration significantly easier.

 

Mapping logic vs. business logic

In section 2.3.  we mentioned the rule of thumb to make production processes generic / plant independent whenever possible. For machine integration, this is only partially true, as there's another trade-off we haven’t mentioned yet. Most production companies acquired their machines over time, leading to the situation where you face very different protocols, communication styles, and machine capabilities. Or in more technical words, every machine has a different API. If you still try to integrate a machine park with a single generic process, you will probably fail, as you would need to add many conditions and exceptions. The grouping will be based on business logic vs. mapping logic, machine types, and lastly, the machine integration protocol itself.

When looking at machine integrations, you usually face some part that describes what should happen. E.g. the machine breaks down, you want to set the resource status accordingly, and the maintenance engineer should be notified. Or if the machine has finished producing a piece, the SFC should be completed, and the component consumptions should be booked. These logics can become quite complex and are specific to the business scenario. We will call it business logic.

In the examples above, we knowingly didn’t mention technical aspects. This is the second part of a machine integration to map the technical protocols. E.g., a specific tag change with path_a for machine_a, path_b for machine_b, and a specific event_e for machine_e all represent the same business event that the machine broke down. This example should show you that as soon as you have a couple of technical integrations, it makes sense to separate the business logic from the tag mapping logic. This will allow you to generalize the business logic aspect of the machine integration while still supporting different production lines, machines, and possibly even protocols.

The tag mapping will either happen in the automatic trigger calling the generalized business process or in separate tag mapping designs, translating the business event into tags, machine events, requests, or the other way around.

 

Machine types

Since we have separated the business logic from the tag mapping, let’s look at different machine types. In production, we usually find different types of machines and integration capabilities. It can range from a torque-enabled wrench, over injection moulding machines offering part counters, to an entire production line with multiple steps, inventory consumption, data collection, and scrap confirmation. As the range is so large, not only the technical mapping varies, but also the business context. Still, as we want to reuse the integration logic, you will typically try to define certain machine types that group some specific machines, which are all integrated in the same fashion and share the same business context. In the following, I will describe an example so you can get a grasp of the concept.

One machine type could be digitally enabled tools, like a torque-enabled wrench, a drill, or an automatic saw. The tools are used by an operator, so probably the start in the POD will be the trigger to begin the operation. Possibly, some machine parameters are handed over to the machine at the beginning of the operation, like a target torque value or the drilling speed. And lastly, there might be some data collection that describes what happened.  The operation itself will be completed in the POD. Going deeper, you would define the business production processes for this on for handing over the set point data and one for capturing the possible data collection. For the data collection, you will need to define how to associate it to the correct operation and so on. When you finish all of this design work, you arrive at your company’s specific definition of that machine type’s integration, which you can then reuse across multiple machines.

Other examples might be the integration of autonomous guided vehicles between work centres, the integration of automatic production lines, and many more. Each type will cover certain functions and take a couple of assumptions about the usage. For defining the types, look at the business functions that should be covered, the triggers for the integration, and also the machine integration protocol to a certain degree. Try to keep the number of types low but still don’t force it as otherwise the type itself will get too complex.

 

Machine integration protocols

DM offers different machine integration protocols that follow different architectural approaches for communication. Here, we want to introduce the most prominent ones and the specifics you should consider For now, the descriptions of the different approaches are rather minimal.

 

OPC-UA ➡️ Tag-based integration

In tag-based integrations, both the machine and the MES can access the tag server as the decoupling point. The OPC-UA tag server holds data tags which could be structured in specific ways e.g. by machine and channel. Both the machine side and the MES side can read and write the tags. DM also offers the option to subscribe to tag changes from the machine via the SAP Production Connector. When integrating the tags or, in DM terminology, indicators, you need to create a representation of the tags in SAP DM as the asset model. In the model, you will need to add every indicator you want to interact with. Only afterwards can you do read and write operations in these indicators.

If you now design your mapping designs, you can use read/write indicator and read/write indicator flexibly. We would always recommend using the flexible option. This option has two clear advantages:

  1.   When you have multiple lines of the same structure. You just need to change the path input to the blocks. With the “normal” read/write, you would need 1 condition arm for each line and a separate block for each line. Making the designs very large and complex.
  2. The second point is closely related. If you design your production processes in your QUAL tenant and later move them to the PROD tenant, you need to rebuild the entire mapping design in the PROD tenant if you use the “normal” block, as the moved design will contain the QUAL indicator references. (This moving point was also one of the reasons for initially separating business logic from the mapping logic, as the separation allowed for moving the business logic part without adaptations from QUAL to PROD.)

 

REST ➡️ Client-server based integration

This type of machine integration is often used when a middleware on the shop floor is present, or if very modern machines offer REST APIs. For the production process design, you don’t need to consider too much.

 

MQTT ➡️ Event-based integration

In event-based machine integrations with MQTT, there is a central MQTT broker that distributes all events relevant to your machine integration or even your entire company. The broker can receive events from all connected parties and distribute them to all subscribers following different service levels. DM can connect to the broker via the SAP Production Connector to both listen for events and publish events. When you choose this style of machine integration, there are two main points you should be aware of.

The first is more relevant for those who have implemented another style of machine integration before and are not used to thinking event-driven yet. In MQTT integration, there is no way to ‘read’ the state of a specific machine. The subscriber, in this case DM, just receives the event whenever the machine publishes an update. Let’s discuss an example to clarify this. Imagine you have a machine that increases a counter every time a piece is produced, sending a message like this {“partsProduced”:20}. To book the yield to the SFC in DM, you will at least need the current operation, plant, resource, and SFC. In an OPC-UA tag-based integration, you might try to retrieve the missing information from other tags. However, in MQTT event-based integrations, all previous events are gone already, so you need to interpret the information in the event. You might check for the active SFC on the resource linked to that event or have the information cached in a global variable. Thus, it’s a different way of thinking compared to tag-based integrations. If you can influence the events published by other participants of the network, it might be helpful to include all the information you need for processing in DM. 

 

Machine Integration Guidelines

In addition to these points on structuring, we also want to provide general guidance on machine integration that is independent of the logic type and technology. We will start with a little summary of the points above and all the later points are in the should category.

  • Try to do less plant-specific logic and reuse as much as possible by having plant-independent generic logic processes for a specific type of integration.
  • Separate machine API interaction from the business logic, as this can be specific to the given machine

Should:

  • Try to minimize back-and-forth communication
    • Send data required for the execution upfront and in bulk (e.g. order setup)
    • Don’t do unnecessary/duplicate validation checks during the WIP execution e.g. checking the SFC status or worker certification; this should be done upfront
    • Ideally, machine integration can work asynchronously and independently of feedback from the partner system
  • Separate the individual pieces of the integration into dedicated processes that can be triggered independently
  • Keep it simple, the simpler the solution, the more robust and easier to maintain
  • Don’t trust the specifications, try and verify the available data points
  • Consider the Machine Cycle Time and the impact of it to your implementation. The longer the Cycle Time the less error prone to network / data retrieval interferences.

 

Caching Data in Global Variables

To round the machine integration of we want to discuss data caching. You may use this approach not only for machine integrations but in all your designs, still it showed to be most useful in machine integrations.

When designing machine integrations, particularly for processes that respond to machine inputs such as increased counters or consumption messages, you may find yourself repeatedly gathering SFC information including resources, work centers, and operations. This repetitive data collection can complicate your processes and make them more time-consuming. If you observe this pattern, consider caching the data. By doing so, you can avoid the need for multiple API calls and instead retrieve the information from a global variable.

When implementing caching we would recommend preferring global variables over local indicators due to their transport capabilities. Further, it is important to be aware of potential overwriting issues, so ensure that you read and write all values for each production line. Lastly, make sure to give some thoughts into the lifecycle of the cache, so it has proper values whenever you use the data.

 

 

DM business function processes

Quick reminder DM business function processes are processes to automate business functions within DM. This could be checks which are triggered from POD, printing, altering, specific rework processes, and so on. These should always be clustered into topic-related designs.

When it comes to rollouts over multiple plants, you mainly need to consider the generalization trade-offs for those processes. In most cases, you should prefer generic processes across plants over individual ones. As you will reduce maintenance effort and speed up the rollout. For sure, the business processes need to be aligned for this to work.

 

 

Next to the structuring, here are the general guidelines for DM business function processes:

  • Prefer event-based trigger over POD-based trigger.
    If you e.g. want to print a label whenever the first operation of a routing is started. You could either adopt the POD and add it to the start button, or use the operation started event to trigger the logic from there. Doing it event-based has the clear advantage that it works for every POD, whether you adopted it or not. If you implement it in the POD, you need to keep track of all adoptions and make sure you don’t move a POD to production that misses the additional function on start.
    Still, sometimes you will need to do it POD-based, as in certain scenarios, you might not have the necessary events available (this is especially true for interceptions).
  • Keep the POD variants to a minimum. As you see in the first guideline logic extensions to the POD will quickly grow in the implementation, but to make sure every piece of logic is applied properly, you should keep the POD versions to a minimum.

 

Business system integration processes

Structuring business system integration processes is quite easy. Those should always be plant independent, as the logic in other business systems, like in EWM, the QM or PM module, or your HR tool like SuccesFactors, will also be plant independent. For sure, you can differentiate whether to activate the logic in a specific plant by setting up the triggers accordingly. This will lead to a constant behavior of DM in your overall enterprise architecture.

 

Optimization for performance

Performance is often a secondary concern during initial development, but as your SAP Digital Manufacturing solution matures and scales, it becomes critical—especially when machine cycle times are tight or user responsiveness directly impacts productivity. This section provides a structured approach to performance optimization, along with practical measures you can apply to your PPDs.

 

Process

Performance optimization should follow a systematic approach rather than ad-hoc tuning. Here’s the recommended five-step process:

  1. Plan performance goals according to your business needs. Start by identifying what matters most. Key factors typically include:
    • UI response times (how quickly operators get feedback)
    • Machine cycle times (can your PPD keep up with production speed?)
    • System load (Are you close to the limitations mentioned in SAP Note 3379404)
  2. Analyse the process to find the critical path. Not every step needs to be fast. Focus on what truly blocks production or the user. Ask yourself:
    • What is most important for production and for the user to move to the next step?
    • Which processes are triggered by production/workers/machines but don’t necessarily influence subsequent steps? (e.g., writing a QM notification back to ERP, informing a third-party system)
    • Which processes can run in parallel? Which have dependencies?
    • When does it make sense to inform the user of progress if multiple long-running activities depend on each other?
  3. Implement performance optimizations. The next section should provide some inspiration on what can be done.
  4. Measure the performance for the identified critical path. Use realistic settings and data setups similar to your production landscape. Run multiple tests to understand execution time variation. à SAP Cloud ALM offers Job & Automation Monitoring, which can be very handy. We recommend activating it and checking it out. No extra license is needed as it is included in all cloud licenses.
  5. Decide if performance is sufficient or if another optimization round is needed. Always consider trade-offs—some performance improvements may reduce maintainability or other quality factors.

If you plan to run load tests in your quality landscape, contact SAP in advance to align the details. Please find the details in SAP Note 3523228

 

Approaches to improve performance

Once you’ve identified your critical path, apply the following techniques where appropriate or create further project-specific approaches. Remember that each comes with trade-offs, so weigh them against your maintainability and readability goals.

Could:

  • Enable fast calls by handing over input parameters directly to the process. Example: In one implementation, the goods receipt was the critical service to enable EWM to continue processing the handling unit (HU). To enable fast goods receipt booking, we pushed additional business data down to the machine layer and provided it as input, so only one service needed to be called before GR booking.
  • Move parts of the process to an earlier point in time. This doesn’t need to be in the same process. Example: Looking again at the goods receipt scenario, we needed a batch to book the goods receipt. Instead of creating the batch when goods are produced, we shifted this work to the start of the order.
  • If using subscriptions, collect everything you would read from indicators and add it to the input parameters. When the subscription triggers the process, all information is provided, and you won’t lose time reading indicators (which are always time-consuming).
  • If you call DM standard read APIs in a subprocess, pass all outputs to the calling process. This allows the caller to avoid duplicate calls of the same API.
  • Don’t call subprocesses from the same design via the service registry; call them as subprocesses directly. You might even try to avoid calling subprocesses altogether, but this comes at the cost of reusability and readability.
  • Consider using asynchronous mode at different levels where blocking isn’t necessary.
  • Consider the sizes of API responses and use narrow filters to ensure small responses where possible.
  • Consider parallelization of services in your design. However, test whether this brings the desired improvement—synchronization at the merging point of multiple parallel flows is usually expensive, so parallelization doesn’t always help.
  • If there is no other way, replace the production process with a custom API you can implement as a BTP service. This is a last resort, as it moves away from low-code design.

 

Reusability vs performance

Throughout this blog series, we’ve emphasized modular, reusable designs. However, reusability and performance sometimes conflict. Encapsulation and reuse often introduce overheads—for example, calling external production processes or subprocesses adds latency compared to embedding logic directly.

Our recommendation: Accept modest delays from external PP calls where they deliver a clear maintainability gain, such as separating machine tag mappings from business logic. For hot paths—the critical steps that directly impact cycle time—consider using in-design subprocesses to avoid unnecessary call overhead. As a rule, prioritize a simpler, testable structure and minimize permutations first, then tune performance once flows and boundaries are stable.

In most cases, you would choose the reusable variant, but if you need the last milliseconds, you may decide against full modularization.

 

Putting it all together into a comprehensive design

A cohesive architecture integrates the three common PPD use cases into a manageable whole. It uses centralized machine integration logic design tailored to different machine types and integration styles to cover business logic, such as announcing orders, starting and completing SFCs, or integrating machine status. The designs may access machine endpoints through various wrappers or dynamic calls, depending on the type of integration, to ensure transportable business logic. The DM business functions and system integration processes are provided in generic designs for reuse across plants in different PODs and Automatic Triggers. After reading this blog, the overview graphic should become clearer, allowing you to develop your own version for your implementation.

 

Have fun designing your production processes.

Lukas, Christian & Robert

entry.title: Architectural Guidance: Implementation of CI/CD Best Practices for SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
We are currently in the PoC phase of implementing a CI/CD pipeline for SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration) and are evaluating the SAP Continuous Integration and Delivery service. Before moving to a production-grade implementation, we require expert clarification on the following points to align with SAP’s recommended best practices:

1)Strategic Value Proposition: Beyond standard automation, what are the primary architectural justifications for implementing a CI/CD pipeline specifically for BTP Integration Suite in an enterprise landscape?

2)The "XML Readability" Gap: A major pain point in the CI/CD workflow is that iFlows are stored in GitHub as XML files. This makes code reviews, change tracking, and "diffing" extremely difficult for developers compared to viewing changes directly in the SAP CPI UI. Does SAP have a recommended strategy or toolset to make Git-based iFlow reviews more human-readable?

3)Comparison of Error Mitigation: We currently use cTMS, which effectively reduces manual errors and provides a structured transport path. Aside from automation speed, what are the critical technical advantages of a CI/CD pipeline that cTMS cannot provide?

4)Impact on Developer Experience: Moving to CI/CD adds the complexity of managing Git branches and Pull Requests.Considering the friction created by managing Git branches for iFlows, how does the CI/CD approach specifically enhance developer productivity?

entry.title: Re: Missing Journal in Billing SAP Public Cloud
entry.content: The reason ZTWU is posting to credit is because the account key you have assigned is defined as a credit (revenue) posting. To get it into the debit column, you need to assign or create an account key mapped to a debit G/L account in OBYC and link that to ZTWU in the pricing procedure.  Also, it is not clear as to why you set the ZTWU as "Statistical" and also assigned Account Key which is not correct.

entry.title: SAP DatabricksでAI Agentを作ってみた
entry.content:
SAPデータを利用して気軽に質問に答えてくれるAI Agentを作りたい！Jouleから質問してSAPデータについて分析をしてほしい！と思ったことはありませんか？本ブログでは、SAP Databricksを利用したJoule用のAPI Endpointの作成方法をご紹介します。

今回のブログを実行していただくことで、SAPデータを利用してDatabricksでAutoMLを利用した予測を作成し、その予測を利用したAIチャットが可能なエージェントを作成することが可能です。外部のAIツールからもこのエージェントを呼び出せるようになります！

本ブログは二部構成となっております。

1. SAP Databricksを利用した予測の作成・APIエンドポイントの作成

2. SAP BTPでアプリケーションを作成・Joule Skillの作成

前提条件

  • インターネット接続環境
  • Webブラウザ（Chrome、Firefox、Safari推奨）
  • GitHubアカウント（リポジトリアクセス用）
  • SAP DatabricksもしくはDatabricksのアカウントへのアクセス

手順

※今回はハンズオン用のデータを利用します。SAP Databricksをご利用の方は、ご自身の環境のデータプロダクトを利用して同様のAI Agentの作成が可能です。 

①教材リポジトリのクローン

  1. 左サイドバーの「Workspace」アイコンをクリック

  2. ツリー構造で「Users」→「あなたのメールアドレス」フォルダを展開

  3. あなたのフォルダを右クリックし、コンテキストメニューから「Create」を選択し、「Git folder」を選択

  4. Create画面で以下を設定：

    • Git repository URL:
      https://github.com/ureshiii-yu/dbx_handson
       
    • Git provider: 「GitHub」を選択
    • Git folder name: dbx_handson（自動入力）

  5. 「Gitフォルダを作成」ボタンをクリック

  6. クローン完了後、左サイドバーのWorkspaceツリーにdbx_handsonフォルダが表示されることを確認

②データ準備の実行

  1. Workspaceからdbx_handsonフォルダを展開

  2. 「0. データ準備」ノートブックをクリックして開く

  3. ノートブック画面右上の「Connect」ボタンをクリック

  4. コンピュートオプションから「Serverless」を選択し、「Connect」をクリック

  5. コンピュートの接続完了を待機（ステータスが緑色の「Connected」になるまで）

  6. ノートブック上部の「Run all」ボタンをクリック

  7. すべてのセルの実行完了まで待機（各セルの左側に緑のチェックマークが表示）

③データ作成の確認

  1. 左サイドバーの「Catalog」アイコンをクリック

  2. カタログブラウザで以下の構造が作成されていることを確認：

    • workspace → default → テーブル一覧
    • 作成されたテーブルが表示されていることを確認
       

④特徴量テーブルの作成

  1. Workspaceに戻り、「1. AI分析」ノートブックを開く

  2. ノートブック右上の「Connect」ボタンからServerlessコンピュートをアタッチ

  3. ノートブックの最初のセル（特徴量テーブル作成用）を実行

    • セル左上の「▶」ボタンをクリック、またはShift + Enter

  4. セル実行完了を確認（緑のチェックマーク表示）

  5. 2つ目のセルを実行し、テーブルにデータが入っているか確認する

⑤AutoMLの実行

  1. 左サイドバーの「Experiments」アイコンをクリック

  2. 「Forecast」ボタンをクリック

     

  3. AutoML設定画面で以下を入力：

    Data Configuration:

    • Training dataset: 手順6で作成した特徴量テーブルを選択 例: workspace.default.feature_sales_by_product
    • Time column: FISCAL_YEAR_MONTH
    • Forecast frequency: Monthly
    • Forecast horizon: 3 を入力
    • Prediction Target column: 売上データの対象列を選択 例:TOTAL_NETAMOUNT
    • Prediction data path: workspace.default（任意）
    • Prediction Table name: forecast_predictions_ureshino（任意）
    • Model registration Register to location: workspace.default（任意）
    • Model name: forecast_model_ureshino（任意）
       

    Advanced options を展開

    • Experiment name: forecast_experiment_ureshino（任意）
    • Time series identifier columns: REGION
    • Holiday region: Japan
    • Timeout: 15 minutes
    • そのほかの項目は自動入力のまま
       

  4. 「Start AutoML」ボタンをクリック

  5. AutoMLの実行開始を確認し、完了まで待機（約15分）
    Databricks のAutoMLでは、様々なモデルで予測を作成し実行結果を比較して確認することができます。

  6.  

  7. 自動的にPrediction Table name　ができているか確認する
     

⑥結果確認関数の作成

  1. 「1. AI分析」ノートブックに戻る

  2. 結果確認用の関数セルを実行（約5分）

    • 「地域別の売上予測を返す関数」のセルの中のコメントアウト部分に予測結果のテーブル名を入力し、コメントアウトを外す
    • 例) FROM workspace.default.forecast_predictions_ureshino
    • 「地域別の売上予測を返す関数」のセルの「▶」ボタンをクリック
    • 「指定された期間の月別・プロダクト別の売上のテーブルを返す関数」のセルの「▶」ボタンをクリック
    • 残りのセルの「▶」ボタンを順番にクリック

  3. 関数作成完了を確認 

⑦Playgroundでの関数設定

  1. 左サイドバーの「Playground」アイコンをクリック

  2. 必要に応じてLLMエンドポイントを変更(任意)

  3. Playground画面右側の「Tools」セクションを確認

  4. 「Add Tool」または「+」ボタンをクリック

  5. ツール選択画面で、UC Functionを選択し、手順8で作成した関数を選択 

  6. 「Add」ボタンをクリックして関数を追加

⑧初回分析の実行

  1. Playgroundのチャット入力欄に以下の質問を入力：
2023年4,5,6月の地域別・製品別の売上についてレポートを作成しなさい

  1. 「Send」ボタンまたはEnterキーで送信

  2. AIが関数を使用してデータ分析を実行し、レポートを生成することを確認

     

⑨予測分析の実行

  1. AutoMLの実行が完了したことを確認（手順⑤）

  2. Playgroundで以下の質問を入力：

2024年4,5,6月の地域別・製品別の売上についてレポートを作成し、著しい増減があれば指摘しなさい。また2024年7月の売上予測を加味して、特に注意が必要な地域を指摘してください。

  1. 「Send」ボタンで送信

  2. AIが売上データの分析と予測結果を組み合わせた包括的なレポートを生成することを確認 

      

⑩AI AgentのAPI Endpointの作成

  1. 「コードを取得」を押し、「エージェントノートブックを作成する」を選択
  2. 必要に応じて 「Register the model to Unity Catalog」セルにある catalog, schema, model_nameを変更する
  3. 「すべてを実行」を押し、Notebookを実行する
  4.  「サービング」を押し、API Endpointが作成されたことを確認
  5. 作成したエンドポイントをクリックする。コピーボタンを押し、API Endpointをコピーする
  6. 外部のシステムからこのエンドポイントを利用する場合、必要に応じてプロフィールからトークンを作成する 

以上で手順は終了です。お疲れ様でした。

次回のブログでは、今回作成したAPI Endpointを利用して、Joule Skillを作成し、JouleからSAP Databricksの予測を呼び出します！お楽しみに🙂

最後までお読みいただきありがとうございました。
